speaker: interviewer
text: And this in an interview with Miriam Buks. In 1939, you got to the Italian border, and you went to the city of Trieste?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you began telling us about a Romanian gentlemen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in May of 1940. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We went to the border to Benghazi. We were supposed to go from there to Palestine-- illegal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's Benghazi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benghazi is Libya. It was then occupied by the-- the Italians-- it was an Italian occupation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were stuck there. The men disappeared, and the boat didn't come. And we were stuck there for, I think, for three months we were in Benghazi. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Then the war broke out. They were-- and the Italian got into the war, because the Italian was not in the war, yet. But in 1940, Italian went in the war with Hitler-- Mussolini. And we were stuck there for three months. I think it was three months. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then we went-- they send us to [INAUDIBLE], was not far from Benghazi. We were there a few weeks, and finally, they send us back to Italy-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Italians-- they sent us back to Naples. In Naples, we were 301 persons on that boat. What we supposed to go illegal to Israel, back then it was Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they send us back to Italy, to Naples. And they put us in jail. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And systematically, they send us to Ferramonti. It was a camp of prisoners of war. They send us to Ferramonti. And in Ferramonti, were for four years. And from there, it was still the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- we got visas to Palestine, and we went to the Polish border. A big-- a very big boat, to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in Ferramonti?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like for you for those four years in Ferramonti?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't bad. You know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they didn't-- there were German Jews, Polish Jews, Hungarian Jews. It was barracks. And was 29 beds in a barrack, or 30 beds in a barrack-- separate for men and separate for women. Families with-- that had children, they got their own room. And they did give us, every nine days, the 10 liras. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they had the little store there. Whatever they had, we could buy. Bread was a ration, but it was countryside. And the-- the people who lived there, our people, they made restaurants, whatever they can get, they cooked. It was hard to get food. While [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Italy was in the war, and it was there, I was there for four years. From there, it was May '44, when I came to-- it's in the passport there, I think, when I came to Palestine. Then we went legal. We got the papers. It was still England-occupied.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you from the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Palestinians, Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Ferramonti?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was the Israeli. Ferramonti? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From Ferramonti. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- well, I don't remember exactly where we went. How we came to-- but I know we landed in-- the boat landed in Egypt, in Alexandria. And there, there were buses from the Jewish committee. They took us to the train. There was a train in the desert. They had refreshments. It was the HIAS of the Jewish organization. They had refreshments in there with the train. It was two days, I think, on the train. We went to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was everyone who was on the boat with you from Ferramonti or did they come from all over?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not everybody, but most of them. I don't know how many people it was-- how many it was when we went illegal. Then, I know it was 301 persons. But the other, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we went already legal, and that was [INAUDIBLE]. How is the name-- what the immigrants came in, in Palestine? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oh, he cannot talk? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Aliya?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a place where the immigrants used to come. And from there, they send them wherever they wanted to go. You have to land there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't remember-- not Eilat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Haifa?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not far from Tel Aviv-- between Haifa and Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: Akhmim?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. He says it's OK. [LAUGHTER] Well, and then I landed in Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And in Palestine, it was already everything OK. We didn't see any war. There was no war, no-- but it was in-- in English occupation. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There I went to live in [NON-ENGLISH] in Haifa and Hadar. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I started work. In the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on the Hadar was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a hospital, the Hadassah hospital. But that was not the Hadassah hospital-- it was a unit from there. It was a maternity ward. I worked there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work when you were in Ferramonti?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ferramonti, I took care of a Italian little girl, two years old. It was the officer's. He was the director from the camp, an Italian couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you treated better or differently than other people because you had that position?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not exactly. Everybody was treated nice. It wasn't like in the German camp. We couldn't go out, but we could walk around openly, and the camp was a very big camp. And they used to bring in wine. They used to bring in fruit. Whatever they had, the farmers brought in and sold it. They had money. Everybody had money. They were German refugees. They had-- they had lots of money. They just need to spend it. And-- but no, everybody was treated all right. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And from there, I went to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Haifa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in the beginning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I came to Haifa, I worked in a little restaurant in Bat Galim. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And my husband lived there, in Bat Galim. And he used to come into that restaurant to eat. And the-- the lady, the owner from that restaurant, she asked me from where I am? From Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we were talking, you know? And I told her, I didn't say from Lubien, I said from [POLISH], because Lubien-- I don't think that was on the map. So I said from [POLISH]. And she said, oh here comes in a man to eat. He's from [POLISH]. Which I knew him from-- from-- from before. Maybe I just didn't know him personally, but I know him. I had a kind of idea that I saw him there in [POLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started to court. He was alone. I was alone. And we court for quite a while, and then we got married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we came to the United States in 1950. My-- my husband's sister brought us here. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your husband come to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing-- he lived in Paris, and he escaped to Morocco. And the same thing-- like everybody wanted to go to Palestine, because it was the only way to go. It was the same thing. He was an immigrant. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers and sisters? Do you know what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know anything. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I wrote them a postal card, but they returned card, so I thought maybe they don't have any money or something. It came back blank. I don't know what happened. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to the United States, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went to Hammond, where my sister-in-law was. They picked us up at the train, and they brought us to Hammond. And the-- the beginning, when we lived in Hammond, my husband was working at Inland Steel. And I used to work wherever I go, whatever I got a job. We didn't have any money. We came and we couldn't take out any money. We didn't have any money. What money we had, we bought things. Couldn't take out $10. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We lived the first few months with my sister-in-law [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and her husband. And then we moved to Chicago, and I was working, [INAUDIBLE]. And my husband was working in Inland Steel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was hard work. Then we bought a store, a shoe repair store in Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we moved to Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in your story, you-- you went to Palestine twice, even though the first time you didn't get into Palestine. Was the passage over different--

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: --the first time, when you went as an illegal immigrant, and the second time legally?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you-- you made passage to Israel twice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't pass to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: You tried--

speaker: interviewee
text: We just went as far-- as far as-- as Benghazi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Were the conditions different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The-- sure it was different. We were strangers and-- and the country-- it was-- you know, it was Jews there, lots of Jews, but all Arab Jews. They weren't Ashkenazi. They were [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Sephardim. They're very good people, very good people. You saw the picture--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the three months in Benghazi, was it hard or was it OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I really [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] don't know how we lived, but it wasn't hard. It's a nice city. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a camp there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in a camp just for a few weeks. Then they put us in the barracks from the-- not barracks, really, but soldiers were. And we stayed there a few weeks, until they sent us back to-- to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took care of you? They gave you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us food. They took good care. There was a war-- it wasn't much for them, either. But as much as they can, it was very reasonable. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Miriam, what is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What picture is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Miriam Buks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really don't remember. I don't know. I was probably about 23 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were living in which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] .

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Miriam, what is this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: From which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Danzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which country issued the passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't understand you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said this was not your Polish passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- it's from Danzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, this is one of the pages from the-- the inside of the German passport. What is this? What was this visa for?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's to-- to go to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go to England, or to go to Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the passport was made to go to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, and this visa?

speaker: interviewee
text: The visa was-- there is a visa from England, and a visa from-- from--

speaker: interviewer
text: From?

speaker: interviewee
text: Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ferramonti.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this picture is made in Benghazi, with a girl from the-- a Jewish girl from there, what lived there. How do you say it? A-- a native there from Ferramonti. Not Ferramonti, Benghazi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm on the left, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's of the store-- Charles Shoe Repair Shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Charles was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that his name in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Yehiezkel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he changed it when he came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which city is this store in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Miriam, what is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, probably in the 1950s. I don't remember the date, exactly. Maybe it's on the other side, written down, but I always forgot to write down the dates.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of you and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: From what period?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same-- it's about the same time, 1950s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it taken in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your husband die?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1967, in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here's a picture with a lot of people in it. Can you tell us who they are? Are you in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I am here. That little girl was Bobbi's daughter, Tomar.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're on the left in the flower dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm on the left. Yeah, yeah. There is my sister-in-law right next to me. In the middle is the  little girl that's Tomar. That's me, and that's my sister-in-law. And it's the whole family. You want me to--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken at the bar mitzvah for Bobbi's younger son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is Bobbi's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bobbi's family. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Am I right, Bobbi?

speaker: interviewer
text: Miriam, would you please introduce who's sitting next to you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my niece, grandniece, Bobbi Winter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, I forgot your-- your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Your maiden name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Marks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Marks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is your husband's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Great-grandniece. Her mother is Sarah Marks Levy. I have a very fine family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Miriam, you're almost 92 years old, and you've lived in a lot of countries, and you've gone through a lot of experiences. Is there anything you would like future generations of Jews to learn from some of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. My head is full of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's so different. You can hardly explain it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll give Bobbi a chance to talk. And then maybe you'll want to add something.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you've--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Miriam's the matriarch of our family, and we all love her. And she used to come to all of our simchas before it became difficult to travel. And we just had a wedding in October, and we missed her. We had my mother's 80th birthday in Florida, and we missed her. And my niece is getting married, her great-great-niece is getting married next year, and we hope that you'll be with us in spirit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in spirit, sure, [LAUGHTER] not in person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we'll see what we can do to get you down to the wedding. So we all love Aunt Miriam. Everybody, all the great-great nieces and nephews write to her, call her. She keeps on a great correspondence. Loved by everybody, and she's the-- the rock bed of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to add, Miriam? Is there anything you try to teach your great-grandchildren--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bedrock of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: --great-grandnieces and nephews? Do you ever discuss your experiences with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with Bobbi--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I made her. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: She made me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I brought my tape recorder.

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell you the truth, I don't even like to talk about it. I have it in my mind all the time, but it's hard to talk about it. There are things you want to bury. But Bobbi-- she came, and she [LAUGHS] made me do it. Bobbi can do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it's important for other people to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when Aunt Miriam and Uncle Yehiezkel came to this country in 1950. Wasn't it 1950?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: How excited my grandmother was-- her one surviving sibling-- she was able to bring over. She wasn't able to save anybody else in her family. How excited we were that they were coming, and how the family immediately adopted them, as if we had never been without them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you both.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you, too.
